overview: The Properties Filter processes Java `.properties` and `.lang` files based on the `java.util.Properties` specification, with additional compatibility features for other property file variants. Each entry's key becomes the text unit name, the value becomes the source content, and preceding comments optionally become translator notes. Note that XML-based Java properties should be processed with the XML Filter or XML Stream Filter instead.

identification.useLd.help:
Enables recognition of localization directives — special comments that override default extraction behavior per-entry. Directives take precedence over key conditions.

If not set, any localization directives in the input file are silently ignored.

extraction.localizeOutside.help:
Controls whether items **outside** the scope of any localization directive are extracted.

Selecting to extract or not outside directives lets you mark up fewer parts of the source document — you only need directives for the exceptions.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this setting to have effect

extraction.useKeyCondition.help:
Enables key-based filtering of which entries to extract. When set, only entries whose key matches (or doesn't match) the regex pattern are extracted.

**Note:** Localization directives have precedence over key conditions.

dependsOn.condition: directives override key conditions when both are active

extraction.keyCondition.help: A regular expression tested against each entry's key. Only entries whose key matches (or doesn't match, depending on `extractOnlyMatchingKey`) are extracted.
extraction.keyCondition.values: A valid Java regular expression pattern.

extraction.extractOnlyMatchingKey.help:
Controls the action taken when a key matches the regex pattern:

- **Enabled** — extract **only** items with keys matching the pattern
- **Disabled** — extract all items **except** those with keys matching the pattern

extraction.extraComments.help:
Recognizes additional comment styles beyond strict Java property comments (`#` and `!`).

When enabled, also recognizes:
- Lines starting with `;`
- Lines where `//` is the first non-whitespace sequence

**Note:** `//` after a `=` is considered part of the entry value, not a comment.

notes.commentsAreNotes.help: Includes comments preceding each entry as a note property on the corresponding text unit. All comment lines before an entry are grouped into a single note.

output.convertLFandTab.help: Converts the escaped sequences `\n` and `\t` in extracted text to actual line-break and tab characters. All other escaped characters remain escaped.

identification.idLikeResname.help:
Uses the property key as the text-unit ID value. The key is already available as the `resname` (name property) — this option copies it to the ID as well.

**Caution:** Key values may not be valid identifiers in all output formats. For example, XLIFF 2 requires the unit ID to be a NMTOKEN value.
notes: The key value may not be a valid string for some output formats — e.g., in XLIFF2 the unit ID must be a NMTOKEN value.

output.escapeExtendedChars.help:
Converts all characters above U+007F into `\uHHHH` Unicode escape sequences in the output.

When disabled, only characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped.

output.useJavaEscapes.help: Retains Java property escape conventions (e.g. `\uHHHH`) in extracted text rather than converting them to their character equivalents.

inlineCodes.enabled.help:
Applies the specified regular expressions to extracted text, converting matches into inline codes.

The default pattern matches:
- `printf`-style format specifiers (`%d`, `%s`, `%1$s`, etc.)
- Escaped whitespace sequences (`\n`, `\r\n`, `\t`, etc.)
- Java `MessageFormat` placeholders (`{0}`, `{1,date}`, etc.)

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Regular expressions defining which text patterns to treat as inline codes. Each rule is a regex tested against extracted content — matches become non-translatable inline codes.

The default expression covers three common patterns:

```regex
((%(([-0+#]?)[-0+#]?)((\ d\$)?)(([\d\*]*(\.[\d\*]*)?)[dioxXucsfeEgGpn])
|((\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v)
|(\{\d.*?\}))
```

processingNotes: **Input encoding:** If the file has a Unicode BOM, that encoding is used; otherwise the default encoding specified in filter options applies.
**Output BOM handling:** For UTF-8 output, a BOM is written only if the input was also UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Transcoding from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 never produces a BOM.
**Line breaks** in the output match the original input line-break style.
Each property entry maps to a text unit: key → name (`resname`), value → source content, preceding comments → note (if enabled).

examples.title: Basic properties file
examples.description: A simple Java properties file showing key-value entries with comments. Both `=` and `:` separators are supported.
examples.input:
```properties
# Example of Java properties

labelOK= OK
msgBadFile: Invalid input file
```

examples.title: Key condition filtering
examples.description: Using the key condition `.*text.*` with **Extract only matching keys** enabled. Only entries whose key contains "text" are extracted — `key1` is skipped.
examples.config:
```yaml
extraction:
  useKeyCondition: true
  extractOnlyMatchingKey: true
  keyCondition: ".*text.*"
```
examples.input:
```properties
key1 = Text for key1
text.err1 = Text for text.err1
menu_text_file = Text for menu_text_file
```
examples.output:
```
Extracted: "Text for text.err1", "Text for menu_text_file"
Skipped:   "Text for key1"
```

examples.title: HTML sub-filter configuration
examples.description: Use the `okf_properties-html-subfilter` configuration (or set the sub-filter to `okf_html`) to process HTML markup embedded within property values through the HTML Filter.
